Abstract Image composition involves integrating foreground ob- ject into background image to obtain a composite im- age. To improve the shadow geometry, we propose to assist diffusion-based methods with geometry priors. Recently, diffusion-based methods have shown superior performance compared to GAN-based methods in shadow generation.

introduction_structure.evidence_text:
1. Introduction
Image composition [25], which aims to appropriately in-
sert a given foreground object into the target background
image to obtain a composite image, serves a crucial role
in fields such as filmmaking, photo editing, and digital art
design [27]. To achieve a more natural-looking composite
result, generating realistic shadow for inserted foreground
objects remains a challenging task, which has not been sat-
isfactorily addressed by existing methods. In this paper, we
focus on the above shadow generation task, aiming to sub-
stantially improve the quality of generated shadow from a
geometric perspective.
The existing shadow generation methods can be di-
vided into GAN-based methods [11, 17] and diffusion-
based methods [20]. GAN-based methods ensure the pre-
cision and authenticity of generated shadow through the
combined supervision of reconstruction loss and adversar-
ial loss. However, they generally require training the model
from scratch on limited paired data, which restricts the gen-
eralization ability. In contrast, diffusion-based methods ini-
tialize the foundation models with rich prior knowledge,
thus significantly outperforming GAN-based methods and
genera

ground shadow given a composite image. On one hand, we
predict the rotated bounding box enclosing the foreground
shadow. On the other hand, we discretize shadow shapes
and retrieve the matched shadow shapes for the foreground
shadow. Specifically, we train an auto-encoder to recon-
struct shadow shapes and extract the bottleneck features as
shape embeddings. We group shape embeddings into clus-
ters and each cluster is associated with a prototypical shape
embedding. Given a composite image, the geometry en-
coder predicts the probabilities of all clusters, indicating the
matched shadow shapes for the foreground shadow.

ing box and matched shape embeddings from the first stage
into diffusion model. Such integration ensures that the gen-
erated shadow not only aligns with the expected spatial con-

tics, thereby enhancing the overall quality and realism of
the shadow. Specifically, we use ControlNet [50] as the
base model. The rotated bounding box is converted to a
binary mask and appended to the input of Control

problem_expression: Introduction Image composition [25], which aims to appropriately in- sert a given foreground object into the target background image to obtain a composite image, serves a crucial role in fields such as filmmaking, photo editing, and digital art design [27].
limitation_expression: However, they are still struggling to generate shadows with plausible geometry in complex cases.
However, they generally require training the model from scratch on limited paired data, which restricts the gen- eralization ability.

The diffusion-based method [20] is adept at generating shadow with realistic appearance ( e.g., shadow intensity, boundary transition), but still performs poorly in terms of shadow geometry ( e.g., location, scale, shape) in the challenging cases as shown in Figure 1.
method_expression: To improve the shadow geometry, we propose to assist diffusion-based methods with geometry priors.
We conduct experiments on DESOBAv2 [20] and real composite images to validate the effectiveness of our method.

In summary, our contributions are as follows: • We propose to equip diffusion-based shadow generation model with geometry priors, which is a key point to en- hance the shadow quality. • We design a multi-task geometry encoder to predict the rotated bounding box and retrieve the matched shadow shapes for the foreground shadow. • The experiments results show that our method brings no- table improvement for the shadow geometry, which has never been achieved by previous methods.
Exper- iments on both DESOBAv2 dataset and real images demon- strate the superiority and generalizability of our method.

Additionally, we inject the matched shape embeddings into the denoising U-Net.
As a result, our approach generates shadow with more accurate position and more precise shapes compared with the state-of-the-art diffusion-based method SGDiffu- sion [20] (See Figure 1).

By using additional geometry encoder, we predict the rotated bounding box and matched shape em- beddings for the foreground shadow, which dramatically en- hances the geometric accuracy of generated shadow.
Conclusion In this work, we have presented a novel diffusion-based shadow generation model: GPSDiffusion, which leverages geometry priors.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Image composition involves integrating foreground ob-
ject into background image to obtain a composite im-
age. One of the key challenges is to produce realis-
tic shadow for the inserted foreground object. Recently,
diffusion-based methods have shown superior performance
compared to GAN-based methods in shadow generation.
However, they are still struggling to generate shadows
with plausible geometry in complex cases. In this pa-
per, we focus on promoting diffusion-based methods by
leveraging geometry priors. Specifically, we first predict
the rotated bounding box and matched shadow shapes
for the foreground shadow. Then, the geometry infor-
mation of rotated bounding box and matched shadow
shapes is injected into ControlNet to facilitate shadow
generation. Extensive experiments on both DESOBAv2
dataset and real composite images validate the effective-
ness of our proposed method. The code and model are
released at https://github.com/bcmi/GPSDiffusion-Object-
Shadow-Generation.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Image composition [25], which aims to appropriately in-
sert a given foreground object into the target background
image to obtain a composite image, serves a crucial role
in fields such as filmmaking, photo editing, and digital art
design [27]. To achieve a more natural-looking composite
result, generating realistic shadow for inserted foreground
objects remains a challenging task, which has not been sat-
isfactorily addressed by existing methods. In this paper, we
focus on the above shadow generation task, aiming to sub-
stantially improve the quality of generated shadow from a
geometric perspective.
The existing shadow generation methods can be di-
vided into GAN-based methods [11, 17] and diffusion-
based methods [20]. GAN-based methods ensure the pre-
cision and authenticity of generated shadow through the
combined supervision of reconstruction loss and adversar-
ial loss. However, they generally require training the model
from scratch on limited paired data, which restricts the gen-
eralization ability. In contrast, diffusion-based methods ini-
tialize the foundation models with rich prior knowledge,
thus significantly outperforming GAN-based methods and
generating more plausible shadow. Overall, the quality of
generated shadow could be evaluated from two aspects: ap-
pearance and geometry. The diffusion-based method [20] is
adept at generating shadow with realistic appearance ( e.g.,
shadow intensity, boundary transition), but still performs
poorly in terms of shadow geometry ( e.g., location, scale,
shape) in the challenging cases as shown in Figure 1.
To improve the shadow geometry, we propose to assist
diffusion-based methods with geometry priors. Our method
has two stages. In the first stage, we employ a multi-task ge-
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
7603

ometry encoder to predict the geometry information of fore-
ground shadow given a composite image. On one hand, we
predict the rotated bounding box enclosing the foreground
shadow. On the other hand, we discretize shadow shapes
and retrieve the matched shadow shapes for the foreground
shadow. Specifically, we train an auto-encoder to recon-
struct shadow shapes and extract the bottleneck features as
shape embeddings. We group shape embeddings into clus-
ters and each cluster is associated with a prototypical shape
embedding. Given a composite image, the geometry en-
coder predicts the probabilities of all clusters, indicating the
matched shadow shapes for the foreground shadow.
In the second stage, we incorporate the rotated bound-
ing box and matched shape embeddings from the first stage
into diffusion model. Such integration ensures that the gen-
erated shadow not only aligns with the expected spatial con-
figuration but also adheres to realistic shape characteris-
tics, thereby enhancing the overall quality and realism of
the shadow. Specifically, we use ControlNet [50] as the
base model. The rotated bounding box is converted to a
binary mask and appended to the input of ControlNet en-
coder, which can improve the accuracy of shadow place-
ment and scaling in the generated results. Additionally,
we inject the matched shape embeddings into the denoising
U-Net. We train additional cross-attention layers and feed
the matched shape embeddings into different layers of U-
Net, which ensures that the generated shadow is informed
by realistic and contextually appropriate shape information,
enhancing the overall quality and coherence of the shadow
synthesis. As a result, our approach generates shadow with
more accurate position and more precise shapes compared
with the state-of-the-art diffusion-based method SGDiffu-
sion [20] (See Figure 1).
We conduct experiments on DESOBAv2 [20] and real
composite images to validate the effectiveness of our
method. In summary, our contributions are as follows:
• We propose to equip diffusion-based shadow generation
model with geometry priors, which is a key point to en-
hance the shadow quality.
• We design a multi-task geometry encoder to predict the
rotated bounding box and retrieve the matched shadow
shapes for the foreground shadow.
• The experiments results show that our method brings no-
table improvement for the shadow geometry, which has
never been achieved by previous methods.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
Given a composite image Ic with inserted foreground ob-
ject, we aim to generate the image ˜Ig with plausible fore-
ground shadow. The overall pipeline of our Geometry Prior
guided Shadow Diffusion model (GPSDiffusion) is illus-
trated in Figure 2. In the first stage, we predict the geometry
priors using a geometry encoder Eg, which will be detailed
in Section 3.1. In the second stage, we generate realistic
shadow using ControlNet that leverages geometry priors,
which will be detailed in Section 3.2.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this work, we have presented a novel diffusion-based
shadow generation model: GPSDiffusion, which leverages
geometry priors. By using additional geometry encoder, we
predict the rotated bounding box and matched shape em-
beddings for the foreground shadow, which dramatically en-
hances the geometric accuracy of generated shadow. Exper-
iments on both DESOBAv2 dataset and real images demon-
strate the superiority and generalizability of our method.
Acknowledgement
The work was supported by the National Natural Science
Foundation of China (Grant No. 62471287).
